headline: Five minutes to a calmer nervous system
subtext: A breathing pattern shown to lower stress and lift mood, faster than meditation.
steps: Breathe in through your nose, then take a second short sip of air to fill your lungs.
Release everything slowly through your mouth in one long, unhurried exhale.
Repeat for five minutes, letting each out breath last longer than the breath in.
caption:
Your fastest route to calm is not a thought. It is an exhale.

When pain or symptoms stick around, the nervous system often gets stuck in a state of high alert. It keeps scanning for danger, and the body stays braced. The breath is one of the few parts of that system you can steer on purpose, and a long exhale is the signal that tells the brain it is safe to stand down.

A Stanford trial tested this directly. Five minutes a day of cyclic sighing, two short inhales through the nose followed by one long exhale, improved mood and lowered resting stress more than mindfulness meditation over a month.

This matters well beyond a stressful afternoon. In chronic pain, and in fatigue and ME/CFS, fibromyalgia, IBS, tinnitus and long COVID, a sensitised nervous system keeps the alarm running long after any injury has healed. Teaching that system safety, a few minutes at a time, is part of how recovery begins.

Ready to work with a practitioner who understands this? Start at https://neurowaze.health/start-my-recovery

#ChronicPain #Neuroplasticity #NervousSystem #Breathwork #Fibromyalgia #LongCovid #MECFS #Tinnitus #MindBody #Neurowaze
first_comment: The science behind this: Balban et al. (2023), Brief structured respiration practices enhance mood and reduce physiological arousal, Cell Reports Medicine, 4(1), 100895. In a randomised study, five minutes of daily cyclic sighing improved positive mood and lowered resting respiratory rate more than mindfulness meditation. Read it: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xcrm.2022.100895
